data.title: Two Strategic Dividend Picks: One For Dividend Income And One For Dividend Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The latest additions of Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP) and Linde (LIN) to The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio help us to maintain a strong balance between dividend income and dividend growth, while also increasing the proportion of the Materials and the Industrials Sectors. Previously, both sectors were significantly underrepresented within our portfolio. Through these incorporations, we have increased the degree of diversification and reduced the portfolio’s risk level.

Additionally, I believe that both companies align well with the investment strategy of our dividend portfolio, prioritizing companies that are financially healthy, fairly valued, possess significant competitive advantages, and that have an excellent position within their respective industry.

Through the inclusion of Linde, the proportion of the Materials Sector within The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio has been increased from 2.11% to 5.52%. By incorporating Brookfield Renewable Partners, the share of the Utilities Sector has been increased from 0.95% to 4.37%.

It is worth noting that due to these incorporations, we have also increased the proportion of the Defensive Sector (which consists of the Consumer Defensive, Health Care, and Utilities Sectors) from 23.73% to 25.55%, further confirming my investment thesis that the portfolio’s risk level has been decreased.

Additionally, the proportion of non-U.S. Equity Assets has been raised from 5.95% to 8.79%, indicating that we have further diversified our dividend portfolio geographically.

In addition to the above, it is worth highlighting that these incorporations have contributed to decreasing the proportion of the Financial Services Sector from 36.17% to 33.61%, further confirming my theory that we have managed to increase the portfolio’s diversification while reducing its risk level.

It is important to highlight that this portfolio remains an attractive choice for investors that want to balance dividend income and dividend growth and who aim to reach attractive risk-adjusted returns.

Proof of this is the fact that it continues to overweight companies with particularly attractive risk-reward profiles: Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A) (accounting for 6.58% of the overall portfolio) remains the largest portfolio position, followed by BlackRock (BLK) (4.48%), Apple (AAPL), (4.30%), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) (4.00%) and Realty Income (O) (3.91%).

Brookfield Renewable Partners

Brookfield Renewable Partners is a company from the Renewable Electricity Industry that was founded in 1999 in Toronto, Canada. The company currently exhibits a Market Capitalization of $13.98B.

I am convinced that Brookfield Renewable Partners strongly aligns with the investment approach of our dividend portfolio. This is the case due to its ability to deliver both dividend income and dividend growth, its financial health and growth outlook.

Brookfield Renewable Partners pays a Distribution Yield [FWD] of 5.45%, which underlines its suitability for dividend income oriented investors. The company's Distribution Yield [TTM] of 5.38% stands significantly above its 5-Year Average of 4.74%, indicating that it is currently undervalued.

At the same time, the company has shown a 5-Year Distribution Growth Rate [CAGR] of 11.60%, further indicating that it is an attractive choice for those aiming to balance both dividend income and dividend growth.

The Seeking Alpha Dividend Yield Grade underscores the attractiveness of Brookfield Renewable Partners’ Distribution Yield. In addition to an attractive Distribution Yield [FWD] of 5.45%, it is worth highlighting the company’s Free Cash Flow Yield [FY1] of 17.10%, which further underlines its capacity to produce a significant amount of dividend income, making it an attractive addition to our portfolio.

Linde

Linde is a company from the Industrial Gases Industry. The company was founded in 1879 and is based in Woking, United Kingdom.

The inclusion of Linde has not only helped us to increase the proportion of the Materials Sector within our portfolio, but to also increase its dividend growth potential.

Even though Linde currently pays a relatively low Dividend Yield [FWD] of only 1.16%, I believe that the company can strongly contribute to our portfolio’s dividend growth potential within the next years.

This is not only underlined by Linde’s 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 13.94%, but also due to its relatively low Payout Ratio of 35.96%, which suggests room for dividend increases in the years to come.

Additionally, it can be highlighted that Linde has already shown 32 Consecutive Years of Dividend Growth, which underscores its ability to provide our portfolio with potential for dividend growth.

The Seeking Alpha Dividend Growth Grade for Linde further underlines the company’s potential to significantly increase our portfolio’s dividend growth potential. In addition to its 3-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 17.49%, the company’s EPS Diluted Growth Rate [FWD] of 11.61% can be noted, serving as an additional indicator of its potential to continuously raise its dividend.

Even though it is true that Linde’s current Valuation is not low (the company exhibits a P/E GAAP [FWD] Ratio of 34.19), it is in line with its 5-Year Average of 35.06, indicating that the company is fairly valued at this moment of writing.

Additionally, I believe that Linde can be rated with a premium when compared to its peer group, given its financial metrics and growth perspective. An EBIT Margin [TTM] of 26.15% is worth highlighting, which stands significantly above the Sector Median of 10.90%. Its Return on Equity [TTM] of 16.39% is also well above the Sector Median of 5.96%, providing evidence of the company’s financial health.

Additionally, I would like to highlight Linde’s EPS FWD Long-Term Growth Rate [3-5Y CAGR] of 10.57%, which further highlights the company’s long-term dividend growth potential. This makes Linde an ideal fit for our dividend portfolio that aims to balance both dividend income and dividend growth.

The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio

The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio’s objective is to generate income via dividend payments, and to annually raise this sum. In addition to that, its goal is to attain an appealing Total Return when investing with a reduced risk level over the long term.

The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio’s reduced risk level will be reached due to its broad diversification over sectors and industries and the inclusion of companies with a low Beta Factor.

Below, you can find the characteristics of The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio:

  • Attractive Weighted Average Dividend Yield [TTM]
  • Attractive Weighted Average Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] 5 Year
  • Relatively low Volatility
  • Relatively low Risk-Level
  • Attractive expected reward in the form of the expected compound annual rate of return
  • Diversification over asset classes
  • Diversification over sectors
  • Diversification over industries
  • Diversification over countries
  • Buy-and-Hold suitability

The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio after Incorporating Shares of Brookfield Renewable Partners and Linde for the amount of $200 each

After adding Brookfield Renewable Partners and Linde to our dividend portfolio, we have raised the proportion of the Utilities Sector from 0.95% to 4.37% and increased the proportion of the Basic Materials Sector from 2.11% to 5.52%.

This implies that the proportion of the Defensive Sector (which is formed by the Consumer Defensive, Health Care, and Utilities Sectors) has been raised from 23.73% to 25.55%, providing evidence that Brookfield Renewable Partners and Linde’s incorporation have not only helped us to increase our portfolio’s level of diversification, but also to reduce its level of risk, providing a larger share of our portfolio to the Defensive Sector.

After the inclusion of these two companies, the Weighted Average Dividend Yield [TTM] of our dividend portfolio stands at 4.32%, while its 5-Year Weighted Average Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] lies at 7.25%. These numbers demonstrate that our portfolio effectively balances dividend income and dividend growth.

Additionally, it is worth noting that we have further diversified our portfolio geographically, by adding a company from Canada (Brookfield Renewable Partners) and one from the United Kingdom (Linde).

The chart below demonstrates the current composition of our dividend portfolio after the incorporation of Brookfield Renewable Partners and Linde.

The Five Largest Positions of The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio after the Acquisition of Brookfield Renewable Partners and Linde

Berkshire Hathaway

Berkshire Hathaway remains the largest portfolio position, accounting for 6.58% of the overall portfolio.

This is important since I believe that Berkshire Hathaway has one of the most attractive risk-reward profiles in the world. This allows us to perform well in any market scenario, ensuring attractive risk-adjusted long-term results for our dividend portfolio.

We should always remember that the largest positions of our portfolio most contribute to its Total Return. This highlights the importance of overweighting companies that have an attractive risk-reward profile.

BlackRock

After the inclusion of Brookfield Renewable Partners and Linde, BlackRock remains the second-largest position of our dividend portfolio, representing 4.48%.

Like Berkshire Hathaway, BlackRock offers investors a particularly attractive risk-reward profile, and in addition, it effectively combines dividend income with dividend growth. BlackRock pays a Dividend Yield [FWD] of 2.04% and has shown a 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 9.27%. This makes BlackRock an excellent choice to be one of the largest positions in our dividend portfolio.

Apple

With a proportion of 4.30%, Apple is the third-largest position in our dividend portfolio. Even though the company does not provide a particularly high dividend yield, nor does it offer a particularly high dividend growth rate, it remains an important strategic element of our portfolio to ensure strong risk-adjusted long-term results. This is thanks to its low risk level, which is, among other factors, a result of its loyal customer base and its own ecosystem, which significantly contribute to the company’s wide economic moat.

It is worth remembering that Apple is not only among the three largest positions of The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio, but it also remains by far the largest proportion of my private portfolio, which has a strong dividend growth focus while providing companies which are particularly attractive in terms of risk and reward with the largest proportions of the overall portfolio.

Johnson & Johnson

Johnson & Johnson represents the fourth-largest position of our dividend portfolio (with a proportion of 4.00%). I believe that it is strategically important to provide the company with a relatively high proportion compared to the overall portfolio given its financial strength and ability to reduce the volatility of our dividend portfolio, indicated by a 24M Beta Factor of 0.29. This makes Johnson & Johnson an important component to stabilize our portfolio during the next stock market decline. Since we do not know when this will occur, it is important to strategically position our portfolio for such an event.

Realty Income

Realty Income is presently the fifth-largest position of our dividend portfolio, representing 3.91%.

Given Realty Income’s attractive Dividend Yield [FWD] of 4.92% and its 10-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 3.91%, in addition to its relatively low risk level (reflected in the company’s strong financial health and broad product portfolio in addition to its 24M Beta Factor of 0.51), it serves as an attractive defense play within our dividend portfolio.

I believe that the company will significantly contribute to providing us with attractive risk-adjusted returns while maintaining our long-term investment focus.

Conclusion

With the addition of Brookfield Renewable Partners and Linde to The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio, we maintain a strong portfolio balance between dividend income and dividend growth, which is one of the key objectives of our dividend portfolio.

After the inclusion of these two companies, the portfolio’s Weighted Average Dividend Yield [TTM] stands at 4.32%, while its 5-Year Weighted Average Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] lies at 7.25%, indicating an effective integration of dividend income and dividend growth.

This strategic balance between dividend income and dividend growth makes the portfolio an attractive option for a variety of investors: those who prioritize the generation of dividend income and those looking for strong dividend growth potential.

Additionally, we have significantly increased the proportions of the Materials and the Industrials Sectors, which helps us to further diversify our portfolio and to reduce its risk-level.

By incorporating Linde into our dividend portfolio, the proportion of the Materials Sector has been increased from 2.11% to 5.52%, while the incorporation of Brookfield Renewable Partners has elevated the Utilities Sector from 0.95% to 4.37%.

Additionally, the portfolio is suitable for those who want to invest with a reduced risk level, evidenced by its broad sector diversification and the inclusion of companies with low Beta Factors, strong competitive advantages and that are financially healthy.

It is important to highlight that after including Brookfield Renewable Partners and Linde, Berkshire Hathaway (with a proportion of 6.58% of the overall portfolio), BlackRock (4.48%), Apple, (4.30%), Johnson & Johnson (4.00%) and Realty Income (3.91%) remain the largest positions of our portfolio. These companies have a particularly attractive risk-reward profile, and I am convinced they will help us to deliver investors with strong risk-adjusted returns over the long term.

What do you think about the latest acquisitions of Brookfield Renewable Partners and Linde? Feel free to share your thoughts in the comments section below!

data.title: Old National Bancorp: Not Enough Here To Buy
data.description:
Old National Bancorp (NASDAQ:ONB) is another regional bank we cover to get a feel for how the economy is doing on a smaller scale. We cover plenty of regionals, as they give us in-depth insight into consumer and small business behaviors. So far, so good this reporting season. Old National is a stock we called for a buy on in early 2023, but have since moved to neutral and have been there since. We remain a hold, as the bank is performing fine, but not enough to really boost the stock. We do not see a compelling reason to buy shares at these levels. Let us hone in on the key metrics of Old National in the just-reported quarter.

On the top line, Old National Bancorp saw revenue of $486 million, rising 6.5% from last year, and beating estimates by $3.6 million. Now there is some loan growth here. Loans at the end of the quarter were up just 2.7% annualized, but have been choppy the last few quarters. Growth over time is welcome organically, but quarter-to-quarter things are mixed. Deposits are also up to $40.8 billion, up $0.8 billion. One of the items we think is worth noting is that it has been a few quarters, and we are still seeing issues with the commercial side of the business, but they have been more disciplined here. The total commercial loan production in Q3 was $1.7 billion and there was $2.8 billion in the pipeline. We see this as positive.

Now, net interest margin and net interest income have been mostly flat here, which is not necessarily a bad thing, but it is not a good thing either. More justification for a hold. Net interest income ticked up slightly to $397.9 million compared to $394.8 million, driven by loan growth as well as higher asset yields and accretion, but this was partly offset by higher funding costs. The Net interest margin was down a single basis point to 3.32%. This is a solid margin, make no mistake, but the stock is fully priced in our estimation relative to many other banks.

We also like to take a look at the bank's asset quality trends. Nothing here stands out at these levels that makes us want to buy, but we will say we see the macro situation improving for banks going forward. Here with Old National, we see here that there was a sizable loan loss provision expense of $28.5 million in the quarter, though this was down from $36 million in the sequential Q2. However, it is up sizably from $19 million last year. Further net charge-offs were up to $17.5 million or 0.19% of all loans, rising from $14 million or 0.16% of all loans in Q2. Further, 30+ day delinquencies of 0.26% and non-performing loans of 1.22% of total loans. The allowance for credit losses is also elevated to $405.9 million, or 1.12% of total loans, this was up from $392.1 million.

With a lower provision expense, and decent net interest margin, we saw return metrics improved. The return on average assets improved 16 basis points from Q2, while the return on average tangible equity was up 190 basis points to 16.0%. Following suit, the efficiency ratio improved to 53.8%, improving 340 basis points.

Final Thoughts

There is a reason Old National Bancorp stock has been mostly sideways, but as a regional, it has moved with the sector. We think this quarter was mixed. Loan growth has stalled, deposits are holding firm, margins are also stable. Asset quality softened, but return metrics improved. All in all, this is a mixed report, and we think a neutral rating is justified.

data.title: Haleon: Too Vanilla For My Liking - I See Why GSK & Pfizer Wanted Out
data.description:
Investment Overview

On 18th July 2022, the Pharma giant GSK (GSK) completed the de-merger of its consumer healthcare business to form a new entity, Haleon (NYSE:HLN). On Friday, 22nd July, Haleon's American Depositary Shares ("ADS") began trading on the NYSE - each ADS share is worth two ordinary shares traded on LSE.

GSK chose to spin out Haleon in part due to pressure from the activist investor Elliott Investment Management, which took a position in the company and began agitating for change, citing GSK's underperforming share price. The consumer health business was essentially a partnership between GSK and Pfizer, formed in 2019, with GSK owning ~68% of the business and Pfizer ~32%.

GSK's share price remains down 4% on a 3-year basis, while Haleon's ADS stock trades at $9.9 per share, up >10% since its trading debut. At the time of listing, analysts questioned why GSK had not accepted a takeover bid for Haleon from rival Unilever (UL), which valued the business at ~£50bn pounds sterling, or ~$65bn, but given the current valuation is ~$44bn, and Haleon began life saddled with ~$13bn of debt, the decision to list the business could prove to be a wiser decision long-term.

Several Pharmas have opted to spin out consumer healthcare or other business divisions in recent years: Pfizer merged its Established Brands division (Viagra, Lipitor, Lyrica and other well-known brands) with generics giant Mylan to create Viatris (VTRS); Merck & Co. (MRK) spun out its women's health and biosimilars business, forming Organon (OGN); Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) its consumer health division to form Kenvue (KVUE); and Sanofi its drug ingredients business to form Euroapi (0EAP).

Generally speaking, the spin-offs are regarded as getting the thin end of the wedge, as the Pharmas exploit tax loopholes to gain substantial financial windfalls, and saddle the spin-off with massive levels of debt, and products whose revenues have already peaked and are in decline.

The spin-offs' management teams are asked to transition the company into a growth business, and the payoff for investors is typically that the spin-offs begin life with a heavily discounted valuation.

As we can see from the table above, however, on a one-year basis, the spinouts Haleon, Viatris, Organon and Kenvue have all performed better than the parent company for shareholders. They have generally competitive investment metrics, i.e. price to sales ("P/S") and price to earnings ("P/E"), Haleon aside. They pay a higher yielding dividend, and, Organon aside, are not overburdened by debt.

Haleon Under The Microscope - Business & Recent Performance

Haleon markets and sells a range of well-known consumer health products across oral health (e.g. Sensodyne), vitamins and supplements (e.g. Centrum), pain relief (e.g. Pandol, Advil), respiratory health (e.g. Otrivin, Flonase), and digestive health (e.g. Tums, Nexium).

In 2023, the company reported full-year revenues of $14.7bn, up 4% year-on-year, operating profit of $2.6bn, up 9.4% and representing a margin of 17%, adjusted operating profit of $3.2bn, up 11%, representing a margin of 23% diluted and adjusted earnings per share ("EPS") of $0.15 and $0.22, free cash flow of $2.1bn, and net debt to adjusted EBITDA of 3x (figures converted to USD using today's exchange rate).

Across 1H24, Haleon achieved revenues of $7.4bn, down ~0.8% year-on-year, adjusted operating profit of $1.68bn, up 1.7%, and adjusted EPS of $0.12, up 6% year-on-year. Free cash flow was $1.08bn, up from $479m in the prior year period. Net debt leverage fell to 2.9x adjusted EBITDA.

For full year 2024, Haleon management is guiding for organic revenue growth of 4-6%, "high single digit" operating profit growth, with $185m of share buyback allocation remaining. The medium term outlook is for annual organic revenue growth of 4-6%, and for operating profit "ahead of organic revenue growth", with dividend growth "at least in line with adjusted earnings".

Net debt currently stands at ~$10.9bn, down from $13.9bn in July 2022, with >75% of the debt being fixed rate and paying 3.6%, with a duration of 6.9 years (according to Haleon's Q2 earnings presentation).

Analysis: Being Cut Adrift, But Finding Its Sea Legs - Can Management Conjure Up Sufficient Growth?

Both GSK and Pfizer have been selling their holdings in Haleon at a fair clip. This month, Pfizer reduced its holding by 640m shares, selling them for ~$5.1 each for a total value of ~$2.43bn. The company apparently has ~701m shares remaining, for an overall ownership of 15%, while GSK has offloaded all of its stake in Haleon, selling ~1.1bn shares in the company over the past couple of years.

According to TipRanks, Haleon is now 21% owned by institutional investors, and ~79% owned by public companies and individual investors. The fact that Pfizer and GSK have sold so much stock is not necessarily a red flag, as they are pursuing a strategy of developing new pharmaceutical drugs with long periods of patent protection, therefore moving away from consumer health altogether, yet were able to quickly find buyers for their large holdings in the new business. This suggests there is long-term optimism around Haleon's business in the office of VCs, hedge funds, and among retail investors.

In its 2023 annual report, Haleon - whose products can, and quite possibly will be used by almost anybody - outlines some obvious reasons for long-term optimism. A global economic shift towards emerging markets, with "strong buying power driving increased per capita spend and usage in these economies". An ageing population that spends more on preventive care and self-care. A stronger consumer focus on health and wellness. Increasing pressure on global health systems, and "sizeable unmet consumer needs", which can be addressed better than ever using market research driven by better technology.

With that said, Q2 performance shows that, while oral health and vitamins, minerals and supplements ("VMS") divisions grew by nearly 10% year-on-year, and digestive health by 5%, pain relief and respiratory health division revenues by 4.4% and 2.3%, and these are no small divisions - representing $1.3bn and $788m of total revenues in Q2.

The logical argument is that, if Haleon's suite of consumer health products were performing outstandingly well, they would not have been spun out of GSK in the first place. Many of the products Haleon sells are older brands whose peak revenues years are behind them, facing the threat of further sales erosion due to newer, more effective competitors that are better positioned to serve today's consumers.

Haleon does have impressive market penetration - its 2023 annual report suggests it has 11% global market share in oral health, 3.1% in Vitamins, Minerals and Supplements (VMS), 13.5% in OTC pain relief, 6% in respiratory health, and 5.4% in digestive health. However, the company cannot afford to stand still, or the business will begin to shrink - a cardinal sin for a spinout, whose challenge is to grow even while paying off debt, and rewarding shareholders.

Unlike other spinouts, Haleon's CEO Brian McNamara initially maintained that the company will shy away from acquisitions or divestments, but the recent sale of its ChapStick business to Suave Brands, in a $430m deal, and the sale of its nicotine therapy business to Dr. Reddy's (RDY), for $630m suggest a shift in strategy.

Sales must be balanced with acquisitions, however, and the only move that I am aware of in that direction is Haleon's recent decision to increase its stake in Tianjin TSKF Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd (“TSKF”), its joint venture in China, to 88%, at a cost of ~$0.5bn.

CEO McNamara called the move "strategically and commercially compelling" - China is undoubtedly a massive market, but with margin shrinking in the US in Q2, shareholders may be looking for more activity closer to home, and may be disappointed. On the Q2 earnings call, the CEO stated:

We love the portfolio we have. We don’t believe we need to do anything with the portfolio to deliver on our guidance that we have given.

Concluding Thoughts - Is Haleon Stock A "Buy", "Sell" or "Hold" Opportunity Today?

Shortly after listing in 2022, Haleon stock plummeted to a value of $5.6 per share, down nearly 40%, but the recovery was swift - shares were back trading in a range of $8 - $9 by December 2022, and there was little in the way of volatility between 2023 and June 2024.

Since June, however, Haleon stock has generated some momentum, reaching a value of >$10.5 in September, before dropping back slightly to its current value of $9.9.

As mentioned, current market cap is $44bn, and if we compare that to some other healthcare providing companies whose revenues fall between $12 - $16bn, let's say Teva Pharma (TEVA), the generics giant, Kenvue (KVUE), and Viatris (VTRS), their respective market cap valuations are $21bn, $44bn, and $14bn.

Are Haleon and Kenvue overvalued, or are Viatris and Teva undervalued? I'd lean toward the former conclusion. Viatris has a similar business model to Kenvue and Haleon, being a spin-out with high levels of debt and ageing products, but it is a couple of years further down the line in its transition and its pivot towards long-term growth is arguably more compelling than Haleon's.

Viatris has not been afraid to make divestitures and pursue M&A, plus it pays a higher yielding dividend than Haleon, yet it is worth nearly 3x less than Haleon. I am a Viatris shareholder and the experience has provided plenty of frustrations, but I am not sure I would rather be holding Haleon stock. To me, it feels as though Haleon lacks direction.

Haleon is promising steady growth and probably has the better portfolio of products in terms of withstanding sales erosion, but I just wonder if Haleon is doing enough in terms of directing efforts towards growing the business.

Cost-cutting initiatives have been initiated by Haleon management but not with any great conviction to date, in my view, saving a few hundred million, and although long-term revenue growth and margin growth is promised, two of four key divisions are experiencing falling revenues, and profit margins in the US are shrinking - by 175 basis points in Q2.

This is my first in-depth look at Haleon and its business, and if I were an investor in the company, I certainly wouldn't be panicking about losing my shirt. But I also believe that, like Pfizer and GSK, I may be looking to dispose of my holding, and I wonder if more and more Haleon shareholders may start to feel that way.

Q3 earnings will arrive soon, and while Q2 earnings triggered a growth spurt for the share price, I wonder if the recent price pull back is more reflective of the market's view of a somewhat - my research suggests - "vanilla" business?

Personally, I don't especially love Haleon's dividend yield, or its remaining share buyback funds, and while the mid single-digit revenue growth forecasts, with operating profit expected to grow ahead of that rate, are ok, I wonder if management may have to row back on these at some point as there seems to be a lack of innovation and too much reliance, potentially, on oral health and VMS divisions continuing to outperform, without any product mix-up or investment in innovation.

As such, although I am tempted to initiate my coverage of Haleon with a "hold" recommendation, I will downgrade to a "sell", because I view the current valuation of the business as too high. I am not sure Haleon has been fully tested yet as an independent business, and I suspect there may be an over-reliance on existing products, and not enough focus on innovating and pivoting the business for long-term, standalone, success.

data.title: Edenred: Attractively Valued Following The Recent Sell-Off
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Edenred (OTC:EDNMF) is a leading provider of employee benefits and payment solutions for businesses. Its main business segments include Benefits & Engagement, which is the largest, along with Mobility Management and Complementary Solutions, which encompasses payment processing, incentive management, and public social programs. Approximately 60% of the company’s revenue comes from Europe, while Latin America accounts for around 30%.

Historically, Edenred has achieved consistent double-digit growth supported by acquisitions coupled with strong profit margins. However, growth has slowed in recent quarters, including during Q3, raising concerns among investors. Regulatory threats in regions like Italy also contributed to the latest decline in the share price. Nevertheless, management remains confident in meeting the previous EBITDA guidance range for FY24 and has projected at least 10% growth in EBITDA for next year, even under worst-case regulatory scenarios. With a current valuation of 8.2 times FCF, the business appears compelling given its outlook. Despite the associated risks, the risk-reward profile is attractive, leading me to rate Edenred's shares as a Buy.

Highlights from the Q3 revenue update

The company posted an operating revenue of €619 million for Q3, which was up 10.8% year over year, on a like-for-like basis. Though growth was solid, it represented a slowdown versus 14% growth seen in Q2. Float related revenue, which is the interest earned on the customer funds that it holds, was up 18% year over year, reaching €63 million, as it continued to benefit from higher interest rates and a growing float value.

Digging into the performance from the different geographies, year-over-year growth in Latin America remained strong at 16.7%. Turning to Europe, which only grew 6.7%, the Mobility segment in particular, which generates revenue as a percentage of the transaction value, saw lesser demand, particularly due to lower fuel prices. Meanwhile, complementary solutions in its main market, France, was negatively impacted due to the ending of a social program.

Despite the growth deceleration that is evident, the main reason for the negative reaction in the company's share price was the company revealing that an amendment had been filed in Italy on the 23rd of October to introduce a 5% cap on meal voucher commissions paid by merchants in the private sector. Commenting on this issue, the company's CEO stated:

We believe that the cap on commission on mail vouchers is legally not applicable because it will run counter to the capital to fully determine their prices, which is a key element of a healthy and competitive market economy. So based on this legal analysis that were run with the best lawyers we can find, our action plan obviously will be to challenge this potential amendment in front of the Italian court, but also the Italian antitrust authority, but also the European Commission. So that is the plan.

With Italy accounting for 13% of total revenue and a slightly higher percentage of EBITDA, management has estimated a potential annual impact of up to €120 million in a worst-case scenario. This figure compares to the guidance range of €1.245 billion to €1.285 billion for EBITDA in FY24. If this amendment is approved, the changes are expected to take effect starting in July next year. The situation remains highly uncertain, and management is not only pursuing legal avenues but also exploring other strategies to mitigate the overall impact.

Expectations beyond FY24

The FY24 adjusted EBITDA guidance provided translates to €668 million at the midpoint for 2H 2024, which would imply a 9.3% year-over-year growth versus the year ago period. In addition, management has also disclosed that it expects at least 10% year-over-year growth in adjusted EBITDA for FY25. In a more positive scenario where there is no impact due to the changes in the regulations in Italy, management sees growth as high as 12%. This is particularly impressive given the backdrop where interest rates are falling and should lead to lower high-margin float revenue during FY25.

Growth in the medium term is expected to be driven by the acquisitions that the company carried out within the last two years, with the purpose of increasing the amount of value-added services for the customers on its platform. This is illustrated in the image shown above. Notable acquisitions include Spirii, a SaaS based charging station and fleet management solution, as well as the pending acquisition of the Italian energy cards business from IP Gruppo api. I look forward to management sharing more details on their growth strategy at the Investor Update planned for December 3rd.

Another reason the company can achieve 10% growth in adjusted EBITDA next year, despite regulatory and interest rate headwinds, is its operating leverage as a predominantly software-based platform. With organic growth decelerating, I expect management to focus on reducing expenses to support margin growth.

The company's balance sheet remains solid with cash amounting to €3.2 billion and a debt load of €5.1 billion, with an average debt maturity of 3.9 years and leverage ratio of 1.5. This has given management the flexibility to aggressively repurchase shares, especially given the recent share price weakness through its €300 million share repurchase program, which was recently increased by a further €75 million. This is in addition to the annual dividend of €1.1 per share.

Valuation

At of the current share price of €30, Edenred has a market cap of €7.35 billion, based on 245 million shares outstanding. With a net debt of $1.9 billion reported at the end of H1 2024, the enterprise value stands at €9.25 billion. Using the midpoint of management's latest EBITDA guidance of €1.265 billion, the shares are trading at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 7.3. However, I believe the Price/FCF metric is more relevant for valuation. To estimate FCF for FY24, I apply a conversion ratio of 70% from EBITDA, which aligns with the lower end of management's guidance. This points to a FCF estimate of €0.89 billion, which translates to a Price/FCF valuation of 8.2.

Relevant peers such as Corpay (CPAY) and WEX (WEX) which operate predominantly in the US market and have high single-digit growth rates, are valued at significantly higher Price/FCF multiples of around 13. I have even previously covered WEX, and concluded that it was a Buy at the current valuation. I will also point out that stock-based compensation expenses ("SBC") at EDEN is roughly 1% of its full-year revenue, which is much lower when compared to CPAY and WEX where SBC amounts to 2.5% and 5.7% respectively.

Looking ahead into FY25, if the company achieves the lower end of guidance, which calls for 10% growth in EBITDA, this would result in an EBITDA of €1.33 billion, after fully adjusting for the €120 million reduction from Italy. This would result in FCF of €0.93 billion, assuming a similar 70% conversion ratio. According to this, the forward-looking Price/FCF would drop even further to just 7.9. Given the company's dominant market position, its solid balance sheet and promising growth outlook, I consider the current valuation to be very appealing, setting up an attractive risk-reward for investors.

Risks to consider

The most significant challenge facing investors is the potential for regulatory changes to meal voucher systems in other countries, similar to the proposals currently being discussed in Italy. Ongoing discussions, particularly in France and Brazil, focus on introducing changes such as price caps.

Revenue growth is expected to encounter headwinds from lower interest rates, which would also negatively impact profit margins. If the ECB and the Brazilian central bank reduce interest rates more rapidly than anticipated by the market, management may need to adjust expectations regarding float-related revenue. Additionally, with around 30% of total revenue coming from Latin American countries, where currencies can be highly volatile, this could present another headwind for the company.

A risk for US investors is the lack of liquidity in the company's shares listed on the OTC Pink exchange, as the company's main listing is currently on the Euronext Paris.

Conclusion

I believe Edenred's shares have been overly penalized in response to slowing growth and heightened regulatory threats. While I recognize these risks, I find the valuation attractive based on management's outlook. This creates a favorable risk-reward scenario that supports my Buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vista's Impressive Execution Continues, But The Stock Is Only Fairly Priced.
data.description:
Vista Energy, S.A.B. de C.V. (NYSE:VIST), one of the leading oil E&P companies from the Argentinian Vaca Muerta basin, reported 3Q24 earnings today.

I have been covering Vista since October 2021, with a consistent Buy rating that I shifted to Hold only in July 2024 for a 600%+ gain. I still believe the company is the best operator in the basin, but I also think that the stock price already discounts most of the positives in the future.

With a stock price that is up 4% since my July article and results fairly in line with expectations, and with the long-term plan laid out by the company a year ago, my thesis remains unchanged. I still believe the name is a Hold. Still, it is interesting to go over some operational aspects and review the valuation.

Q3 2024 results

Expanding production according to plan: Vista continues drilling on its main properties to close the year at an average capacity of 85 thousand barrels by Q4. The company posted an average of 72 thousand boe in Q3, 12% above Q2 figures and almost 50% above Q3 '23 figures.

Oil prices are resilient: Oil prices (CL1:COM) have been elevated compared to the pre-pandemic period for three years already, and despite recession fears, they are still hovering around $70. As mentioned in previous articles, Brent's (CO1:COM) long-term (2000s onwards) average has been $75. Resilient oil prices, combined with production, have obviously translated into higher revenues and profit expansion.

Lifting costs up, but not that much: The company's lifting costs ($4.7/boe) were down YoY (2%) but up compared to Q2 ($4.5) and even more against 4Q23 ($4.1). The increase is significant compared to 4Q23, but we have to consider that Argentina has inflated significantly in dollars. 4Q23 figures contemplated a US/ARS exchange rate of about 800 versus one closer to 950 by the end of Q3 (so 18% devaluation) versus inflation of 100% for 9M24. This means local costs have almost doubled in dollars, and yet the company's lifting costs are still manageable.

The effect of the increase in local costs is much more evident in G&A expenses, which doubled from $15 million in 3Q23 to $30 million in 3Q24 (adding 1pp of costs).

Transport is becoming a bottleneck: As mentioned in previous articles, Vista's main production bottleneck is transport to consumption (Buenos Aires) and export (Bahia Blanca). The pipelines (mainly OldelVal) are already at full capacity, and expansion projects are expected to be operating in early 2025. Still, they will only add 30 thousand barrels per day to Vista's capacity (about 50% expansion from the current 55/60 thousand).

The problem is that Vista has to increase the use of trucking, which reached almost 20 thousand boe per day in 3Q24 and is expected to peak percentage-wise in Q4 but will still potentially reach 37 thousand barrels in FY25. The issue is that trucking adds $15 in costs per barrel. This is evident in Vista's transport costs (part of sales expenses), which tripled from $7 million in 3Q23 to $23 million in 3Q24.

Updated long-term goals: The company raised its production guidance for 2025 when it expects to reach average production for the year of between 95 and 100 thousand boe (which would imply the end of 4Q25 at about 110/115 thousand boe). The company also expects to reach 150 thousand boe by 2030. This is about the maximum that Vista could reach on its current acreage, considering it has reserves of about 385 million barrels, and a production rate of 150 thousand per day already implies 55 million a year.

Valuation

Model updated: I provided two models for Vista's valuation in my July article. One is based on production costs and provides net income as a function of Brent prices. The second one provides the undiscounted value of Vista's production based on Brent prices. The only update I made was to adjust production for the average FY25 figures to 100 thousand boe per day.

The model is not very far away from Vista's own model because the company guided for an adjusted EBITDA of $1.5 billion at a Brent of $75. Based on the production of 100 thousand boe, the company would post D&A of about $600 million (they posted $115 million in 3Q24 extracting 72 thousand barrels per day), so their calculation of operating income is closer to $900 million, whereas mine is about $1.1 billion.

Market cap and yields as a function of Brent: Vista currently has a market cap of $4.5 billion. In my opinion, a company in Argentina (a country famous for its political instability and lack of economic policy consistency) participating in a cyclical industry should provide a return of at least 15%.

Based on the company's market cap and production in 2025, we need Brent oil prices to be between $70/75 (net income of $675 million) to obtain a 15% yield. This is coincidental in both models.

It is true that the company can keep growing (it expects 150 thousand barrels per day by 2030), but projecting the capital structure and costs so far ahead is much more complex. If the company carried that growth at constant costs, then it would represent earnings growth of about 8.5% per year until 2030. That would mean a Brent price of $55/60 would be ok to obtain a return of 15% because the current yield would be about 7%, and the rest would come from growth.

Conclusion

I really like Vista; it is the best operator in the Vaca Muerta basin, it is unlevered, and it has the best exposure to oil pure-play (versus other companies more exposed to energy generation or natural gas like Pampa or YPF). The company's execution has been impressive.

Yet, I believe the name's good qualities have become priced in. The name offers a sufficient yield only if assuming it can continue growing at high rates for six years into the future (a significant challenge in oil markets and in Argentina), or that Brent prices will remain elevated for a long time.

The growth may be there, based on the company's execution so far, and Brent prices might remain close to $75 (it is close to the historical average). However, that would only represent a fair valuation, not an opportunity. For that reason, I believe Vista's price is fair, but I'm not inclined to buy here, and therefore consider the stock a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Robert Half Inc.: Still Negative On The Near-Term Performance
data.description:
Investment action

I recommended a sell rating for Robert Half Inc. (NYSE:RHI) when I wrote about it in July, as I expected the share price to remain pressured given near-term uncertainties and the lack of a positive catalyst. Based on my current outlook and analysis, I reiterate my sell rating for RHI because of the poor near-term earnings outlook, which I expect to be challenged by lackluster topline performance and negative operating leverage. I acknowledge the potential for a strong recovery over the next two years, but I don’t think the right time to invest is today given the high expectations embedded into RHI’s valuation multiple.

Review

RHI reported its 3Q24 results recently, and performance was not great. Total revenue fell 6.3% y/y to $1.465 billion (though this is better than consensus estimate of -8.2%) on a reported basis. On a same-day constant currency [SDCC] basis, revenue fell by 7.7% y/y in the quarter. While this was slightly better than the 10.1% decline seen in 2Q24, weakness is still apparent across major segments and verticals. Total contract talent solutions fell by 11.9% and 13.2% SDCC. Within it, finance & accounting [F&A] revenue fell by 9.2% y/y, administrative & customer support [A&CS] fell by 9.2% y/y, and technology revenue fell by 6.1% y/y. Permanent placement talent also fell by 11.9%, indicating no signs of recovery (2Q24 fell by 12.2%). The only bright side of these earnings was that Protiviti revenue grew 6.4% y/y, recovering from five quarters of negative y/y growth. Consequently, profit performance did not come in pretty. EBIT margin compressed by 210bps y/y to 6.2% due to negative operating leverage and led to an adj EPS decline of 29.2% to $0.64.

Like I said in my previous update, there is still room for revenue to see y/y decline if we benchmark against the dot-com crisis (RHI saw 10 straight quarters of y/y decline). With the 3Q24 decline, this marked the 8th straight quarter of y/y decline. RHI’s 3Q24 results should put an end to the share price rally and dismiss any potential for near-term recovery. Despite the Fed cutting rates and rather positive economic outlook, these are not translating to strong hiring trends. In fact, the same trend persists, in that businesses continue to delay hiring decisions, and this directly translated to double-digit y/y declines in staffing revenue. Also, similar to last quarter, management pointed out that weakness in hiring sustained into 4Q24, where contract talent solution revenue declined by 12% in the first two weeks of October (this is the same level as the 13% decline seen in 3Q24). Permanent placement talent solution revenue performance is even worse; it has decelerated to a 19% y/y decline in the first three weeks of October vs. the 13% decline seen in 3Q24. I expect this weak trend to continue for the near term as businesses continue to stay wary of the economy (as seen from the US business confidence index), especially with the upcoming uncertain US election.

On a headline basis, 4Q24 is going to look poor as well, given that the quarter has 2.5 fewer days and the winding down of several large projects in Protiviti. This should further weaken the stock’s sentiment for the rest of the year.

The knock-on implication here is the sizeable impact on adj EPS because of the negative operating leverage (we already saw this happen in 3Q24). Management continues to hold on to recruiting capacity, which leads to sizeable decremental margins in a downturn. As a reference, management guidance is implicitly calling for a 280 bps y/y decline at the midpoint in 4Q24.

The one positive aspect that I will point out that may signal a very early sign of recovery is that weekly sequential staffing levels have remained stable over the past 12–14 weeks. According to management, this is the longest consecutive period of stability over the last 24 months, which may suggest a bottom.

Well, so given that all we do is a white collar, white collar is everything. As we said that, we now have the longest period of stability or flatness, we have 14 consecutive weeks of flatness, and we haven't seen that kind of duration over the last two years, which was the period we've been otherwise drifting down sequentially. So the trend, the white collar trend is the overall trend that we referenced, and it's a better trend than we had been seeing. You have to start somewhere. We flattened out. It's more than a week or two. It's more than four or five or six weeks. It's a long time, it's 14 straight weeks. So we're encouraged that we've flattened out for that period of time.

And given the Optimism Index improvements we see from NFIB, given the hope that we get through the election uncertainty relatively quickly, we're optimistic. 3Q24 call

Valuation

Ultimately, the question to answer is when will economic activity pick up strongly, which will lead to an increase in hiring. While I am negative on the near-term, I would say that as the Fed continues to cut rates, the uncertainty impact from the US election goes away, and US GDP continues to grow, we should see a recovery sometime next year in FY26. That will be the right timing to long RHI, as earnings growth will be significant on the back of strong volume/revenue growth and high operating leverage.

I don’t recommend investing today because the near-term earnings outlook is still poor, and the quarter-to-date data suggests that we are in for another poor 4Q24. With the stock still trading at ~21x forward PE (~4x above its through-cycle PE), which implies the market is still hopeful for a near-term recovery, I think the expectations are high. If 4Q24 performs weaker than expected and management guidance/comments for FY25 remain poor (this really depends on the macro situation, which is tough to predict), expectations could reset downwards, leading to a decline in multiples.

Final thoughts

My recommendation remains a sell rating for RHI. The 3Q24 results highlight the continued challenges facing the company in the near term. Weak economic conditions, coupled with the ongoing uncertainty surrounding the US election, should continue to pressure revenue and profitability performance. While there are signs of stabilization in certain areas, I believe the outlook remains weak.

data.title: Very Good News For Big Dividend Infrastructure Stocks
data.description:
Big dividend infrastructure stocks, like the ones in the Cohen&Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF), have boomed recently, as the chart below illustrates. Companies like Williams Companies (WMB), TC Energy (TRP), Enbridge (ENB), Kinder Morgan (KMI), ONEOK (OKE), DT Midstream (DTM), and Targa Resources (TRGP) have surged in recent months.

In this article, I will discuss why the sector has boomed and some of my favorite places to invest in it moving forward.

The Good News For Big Dividend Infrastructure Stocks

Two primary factors are driving the infrastructure stock boom:

First, infrastructure stocks are often considered utility-like in nature and therefore serve as bond proxies. The majority of their cash flows come from assets that are either regulated or under long-term contracts, ensuring stable cash flows over time. This makes them comparable to bonds or utilities. Recently, long-term interest rates have declined due to expectations that the Federal Reserve would cut rates, which has now occurred. As interest rates drop, the net present value of the cash flows generated by these highly contracted and regulated assets increases, driving stock prices higher.

The second, more unique reason for the surge in midstream infrastructure stocks is the boom in artificial intelligence ("AI"). While leading AI stocks like Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Palantir (PLTR) have soared in recent years due to growing demand for AI applications, the focus has recently shifted to the AI infrastructure space. The market now realizes that building the data centers needed to support AI-driven economic growth will require substantial energy.

Although AI companies like Microsoft and Amazon (AMZN) are making efforts to power data centers with renewable energy — such as Microsoft’s recent deal with Brookfield (BAM)(BN)(BEP)(BEPC) and investments in nuclear power with Constellation Energy (CEG) and Vistra (VST) — natural gas is emerging as an essential energy source for these data centers.

Natural gas offers several advantages. First, it provides a more reliable power supply than renewables, as it is not dependent on the sun shining or wind blowing. While battery storage can supplement renewables by providing a more consistent source of power for data centers, it adds extra cost and complexity to the process. In contrast, natural gas power plants offer a straightforward power solution for data centers. Additionally, natural gas power can be quickly ramped up to meet sudden spikes in electricity demand, which is essential for managing the complex workloads of AI models. In contrast, this level of flexibility is more challenging to achieve with renewable energy and even with nuclear power. Natural gas is also relatively inexpensive and cleaner than other fossil fuels like oil and coal, making it an attractive choice for companies that want to balance reliability and flexibility with environmental considerations. As a result, it is expected that natural gas demand will grow considerably in the years to come.

This anticipated growth in natural gas demand has been a significant boost for midstream infrastructure companies, many of which have extensive natural gas infrastructure across North America. Furthermore, numerous existing and planned data centers are located near this infrastructure, making these companies well-positioned for growth in the coming years while also giving them a moat of sorts.

Investor Takeaway

So, what are the best ways to invest in midstream infrastructure right now? Although many midstream infrastructure stocks have surged in recent months, some are still trading at near fair value, making them solid portfolio diversifiers — especially for income-focused investors. Among the options available, Enbridge (ENB) and TC Energy (TRP) stand out as both companies offer high yields, solid growth prospects, and well-diversified business models, making them attractive long-term investments.

However, some of the best opportunities may lie within the master limited partnership (AMLP) part of the midstream sector. While MLPs can deter some investors — particularly institutional funds that prefer C corporations — their unique structure also creates opportunities for those willing to deal with their K1s.

I particularly like Energy Transfer (ET), which has significant natural gas and NGL assets, and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), which offers strong diversification across the midstream sector. Western Midstream (WES) is another solid option because - though its assets are not located near current or planned data centers - it stands to benefit from the broader increase in natural gas demand.

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC) is another compelling infrastructure opportunity right now. It is involved not only in natural gas infrastructure, but also in data center infrastructure, positioning it to benefit significantly from the AI infrastructure boom.

Moreover, as demand for natural gas rises, it will likely have a ripple effect across the broader energy sector as growing natural gas prices (due to strong data center demand) may make oil more attractive for certain applications currently powered by gas, further driving demand for oil. As the saying goes, a rising tide lifts all boats, and I believe now is an excellent time to invest in midstream infrastructure and energy (XLE) in general, given that in many cases energy stock prices have lagged the broader market.

data.title: SLQD: Short Duration Bond Fund From iShares
data.description:
Thesis

iShares is very good at covering all the large building blocks in the financial markets. The ETF provider has staple names in the equity and fixed income markets, names which have enormous liquidity and are traded as proxies for the actual underlying holdings by institutional investors. On the investment grade fixed income side, the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) is the best-known product, with a $27 billion AUM and ample daily liquidity. LQD addresses the need to access the IG bond universe via an ETF and represents a take on an average IG bond portfolio, including its high duration of 8.5 years.

In order to address the shorter end of the IG bond curve, iShares created the iShares 0-5 Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (NASDAQ:SLQD) with the nomenclature reminding us of 'short-dated' LQD.

In this article, we are going to take a look at the ETF, its features and characteristics and opine on why the name is a buy in today's macro environment.

Fund composition - short-dated IG bonds

As per its own definition, SLQD purchases shorter-dated IG bonds:

The iShares 0-5 Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of U.S. dollar-denominated, investment-grade corporate bonds with remaining maturities of less than five years.

The ETF comes with an effective duration of 2 years and a granular portfolio:

The portfolio weighted average maturity is 2.24 years and the effective duration is 2 years. The ETF holds over 2600 names and has a low option-adjusted spread of 50 bps.

The fund holdings fall mostly in the A and BBB rating bands:

We can notice from the above table that A-rated names represent 47% of the holdings, while BBB rated bonds are 43% of the portfolio. Given the low fund duration, the credit risk in the portfolio is very limited, absent another great financial crisis. The portfolio is concentrated in the banking sector, which represents 33% of the fund, followed by consumer non-cyclical at 13%.

SLQD is more of an institutional product

Given its composition and holdings, SLQD is a name that will capture the short end of the IG bond curve and thus will not provide eye-watering results:

Expect a 2% total return in a low rate environment, while today's high risk-free rates macrocycle should command a total return in the 5% to 6% range. To that end, retail investors might not buy into this fund as much since returns are very low. Institutional investors, on the other hand, have required allocation amounts for certain sectors and thus might be enticed by the fund's daily liquidity and high $2 billion AUM.

As an investor here one needs to understand the ETF does not take much in terms of credit risk, this is an instrument mostly driven by front-end rates. The fund comes with low historic returns but also very low volatility (the 3-year standard deviation is 3.3%).

Risk driver - rates

The ETF's main risk driver is represented by front-end rates. The fund has a 4.4% 30-day SEC yield, which is composed of risk-free rates and a credit spread. Given that the 2-year treasury CMT yield is roughly 4%, and the portfolio OAS is 50 bps, we can see how the 30-day SEC yield is composed.

If we do a bit of math on the portfolio yield and its components as described above, we can see that risk-free rates represent roughly 90% of the SEC yield. This means that the prevailing risk driver for this ETF is represented by rates. While there is a small credit component, even if credit spreads blow out and triple from the current levels (i.e., a 100 bps move), we can expect only a -2% down move in the fund assuming the CS01 duration is fairly similar to the rates sensitivity.

We are in an environment of very elevated risk-free rates, and the Fed has engaged in a monetary easing cycle which is set to continue despite inflationary pressures. The 2-year rates are tightly correlated with Fed Funds, so one should fully expect yields to move lower from here. SLQD is not a very exciting or volatile name, but is nonetheless set to benefit from lower rates at the front end of the curve.

Base case scenario and risk factors

In a soft landing scenario where credit spreads remain contained SLQD will gain as Fed Funds and 2-year rates move lower. For 100 bps in lower rates, the ETF will see a 2% capital gain plus its dividend yield. In our base case, the ETF will post a return of roughly 6% in the next 12 months.

The main risk factor for the ETF is represented by the re-emergence of inflationary pressures. A high inflation would force the Fed to pause its easing policy or even hike rates, thus putting pressure on the fund NAV. An inflationary recession scenario (i.e., a recession driven by higher rates) would see credit spreads move higher as well, thus potentially moving the name to a -4% drawdown.

Conclusion

SLQD is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name comes from iShares and is a complementary product to the much better-known LQD fund. SLQD aims to follow the short end of the IG bond curve with a 2-year duration and a granular build. The name has a very low volatility profile but also muted historical total returns. We view SLQD as more of an institutional product via its liquidity and niche. The fund comes with a 50 bps OAS on top of 2-year rates and is slated to make roughly 6% in the next twelve months in a soft landing base case scenario. Rates represent the main risk factor for this name, and from a macro standpoint, we are due to move lower here as the Fed cuts.
